perturbation.original_text: Recipient agrees that all Confidential Information received is and will remain the sole property of Discloser. Neither the execut ion of this Agreement, nor the furnishing of any Confidential Information hereunder shall be construed as a gr ant by implication, estoppel or otherwise , of a license by either party to the other to make, have made, use or sell any product using C onfidential Information or as a license under any patent, patent application, utility model, copyright, maskwork ri ght, or any other intellectual property right.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient understands that Confidential Information received is generally considered the property of Discloser. Neither the execution of this Agreement, nor the furnishing of any Confidential Information hereunder shall necessarily be construed as a grant by implication, estoppel or otherwise , of a license by either party to the other to make, have made, use or sell any product using C onfidential Information or as a license under any patent, patent application, utility model, copyright, maskwork ri ght, or any other intellectual property right.
perturbation.explanation: The modification replaces 'agrees that all Confidential Information ... will remain the sole property' with 'understands that Confidential Information is generally considered the property'. This introduces ambiguity, suggesting that the Discloser's ownership might not be absolute or consistently enforced. The use of 'necessarily' weakens the denial of a license grant. This may create confusion about ownership and rights to the confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: Under New York law, the clear and unambiguous ownership of trade secrets and confidential information is vital for its protection. The modified text, by using 'generally considered' and 'necessarily', introduces ambiguity about ownership. This contradicts the need for clear and demonstrable ownership to protect the confidential information, as required to pursue legal action for trade secret misappropriation or breach of confidentiality, weakening the protection the original text gave.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: NYS Open Legislation | NYSenate.gov Sorry, you need to enable JavaScript to visit this website. Skip to main content The requested entry could not be found. Try searching OpenLegislation Browse all Laws of New York
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity regarding the ownership of confidential information, weakening the protection afforded to the Discloser. The original text states the Recipient 'agrees that all Confidential Information received is and will remain the sole property of Discloser,' whereas the changed text states the Recipient 'understands that Confidential Information is generally considered the property of Discloser.' The use of 'generally considered' instead of 'agrees that ... is and will remain the sole property' introduces a significant degree of uncertainty regarding ownership. Similarly, the original text stated that the execution of the agreement shall not be construed as a grant of license. The modified text uses 'necessarily' weakening that denial. According to the Law Explanation, 'the clear and unambiguous ownership of trade secrets and confidential information is vital for its protection'. The modified text undermines this clear ownership. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is not helpful, the Law Explanation provides a legal basis. Therefore, the alteration creates a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement and the rights and obligations hereunder may not be transferred or assigned by one party without the prior written appr oval of the other party hereto.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement and the rights and obligations hereunder may not be easily transferred or assigned by one party without the prior written appr oval of the other party hereto.
perturbation.explanation: Adding the word 'easily' introduces ambiguity as to what constitutes an 'easy' transfer or assignment. This could be interpreted in multiple ways, potentially allowing a party to circumvent the intended restriction on transfer or assignment under certain circumstances.
perturbation.law_explanation: NY Gen Oblig L § 13-101 states about the transfer of claims or demands. Adding 'easily' goes against this general principle by creating an uncertain exception. While the law acknowledges the possibility of transferring obligations, contracts often contain clauses restricting assignment and transfer. The modified text weakens this by opening the door for arguments about what 'easily' means.

Laws (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 New York Laws GOB - General Obligations Article 13 - Transfer of Obligations and Rights Title 1 - Transferability; Effect of Transfer 13-101 - Transfer of Claims. Universal Citation: NY Gen Oblig L § 13-101 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Next § 13-101. Transfer of claims. Any claim or demand can be transferred, except in one of the following cases: 1. Where it is to recover damages for a personal injury; 2. Where it is founded upon a grant, which is made void by a statute of the state; or upon a claim to or interest in real property, a grant of which, by the transferrer, would be void by such a statute; 3. Where a transfer thereof is expressly forbidden by: (a) a statute of the state, or (b) a statute of the United States, or (c) would contravene public policy. perturbation.justification: The addition of "easily" in the changed text introduces an ambiguity regarding the transfer or assignment of rights and obligations, which contradicts the intent of NY Gen Oblig L § 13-101 as interpreted by the LLM. While the law generally permits the transfer of claims, contracts often restrict such transfers, and the original text reflected this restriction. The inclusion of "easily" weakens this restriction, creating an exception that could lead to disputes about what constitutes an "easy" transfer and potentially undermining the original intent of the clause. The scraped snippet 2 references "NY Gen Oblig L § 13-101 (2022) - Transfer of Claims" and it mentions that a transfer can be restricted if it "would contravene public policy." Therefore, by creating an ambiguity, the contract goes against general obligations law.
